main_summary.summary: Robert G. Clark Jr., the first African American elected to the Mississippi House of Representatives in the twentieth century, was interviewed by Dr. John Dittmer on March 14, 2013, at Clark's family home in Pickens, Holmes County, Mississippi. Clark recounts being born in the house his father built on land where his great-grandparents had been enslaved and which the family acquired after emancipation. He describes walking miles to a four-to-five-month grammar school, attending Holmes County Training School (now Williams-Sullivan School) at Durant, and working his way through Jackson College under President Jacob L. Reddix and Dean H.T. Sampson. After teaching at Humphreys County Training School near Louise, Mississippi, he was dismissed for supporting school integration; his father was likewise fired and blacklisted via the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission. Clark earned a master's degree at Michigan State University, returned to Holmes County during Freedom Summer, entered the movement through Henry and Sue Lorenzi, and won a 1967 legislative seat as an independent. He details Marian Wright's defense of his seating, his alliance with Buddie Newman, his chairmanship of the House Education Committee, the passage of Governor William Winter's 1982 Education Reform Act restoring compulsory attendance and adding kindergarten, his two losing campaigns against Webb Franklin for the U.S. House, and his 1992 election as Speaker Pro Tempore.
key_themes: Family land ownership from emancipation through twentieth century
Black educators fired for supporting integration and voter registration
Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission surveillance and blacklisting
Independent legislative election under the post-Voting-Rights-Act franchise
Mississippi Education Reform Act of 1982 and compulsory school attendance
main_summary.historical_significance:
Clark is the sole primary-source witness to the political opening of the Mississippi House of Representatives to Black members after Reconstruction. His account documents the mechanisms of the Sovereignty Commission's school-employment blacklist (operating against both him and his father), the procedural isolation tactics deployed against him during his first eight years as the only Black legislator, and the legislative maneuvering that produced the 1982 Education Reform Act with Governor William Winter. The interview also preserves first-hand testimony on the 1955 Brown-era firings of Black teachers in the Mississippi Delta, the Belzoni shooting of a voter-registration activist Clark places at his own barbershop chair, and the Holmes County Civil Rights Movement's connection to the Lorenzis and Freedom Summer.

As a Black officeholder who served continuously from 1967 through the era of his 1992 Speaker Pro Tempore election, Clark provides a continuous narrative bridging the pre-Voting-Rights-Act educational world of his own childhood (segregated four-month schools, his father acquitted of murder in 1940) and the post-Voting-Rights-Act legislative environment. His account of two campaigns against Webb Franklin documents how racial-coded electoral appeals ("Webb Franklin, he's one of us") operated in 1980s Mississippi congressional contests.

chapters.title: The Only Address I Ever Had
chapters.summary: Interviewed by Dr. John Dittmer in Pickens, Mississippi in March 2013, Robert G. Clark Jr., the first African American elected to the Mississippi House of Representatives in the 20th century, opens in the house his father built about a hundred years earlier, on land where his great-grandparents had been enslaved and which the former slave master sold to his great-grandfather after slavery.

chapters.title: Come Back Home to the Place
chapters.summary: Clark recounts how, dreaming of leaving for Chicago after he finished twelfth grade, he was summoned by his father and his Aunt Annie, who frightened him before asking that he come back and stay on the family place, and how, after arguing hard, he agreed, returning to the plantation where his great-grandfather had been enslaved.

chapters.title: Jailed With His Father in 1940
chapters.summary: Clark describes his siblings leaving for California and Memphis to attend high school the county denied Black students, then turns to the 1940 case Jack Bass recounted in Melany Neilson's book: at eleven or twelve he and his brother were jailed overnight when officers suspected his father in a death found off their property, a white Lexington lawyer winning acquittal on grounds of self-defense.

chapters.title: Four to Five Months of School
chapters.topic: A Childhood Apart From the Plantation
chapters.summary: Clark explains that because the family worked its own land he never labored in a white man's cotton field, which made other Black families see him as different, and that the cotton calendar dictated a four- or five-month grammar-school year, with a three-mile walk through rain, sleet, and snow to a school at the place called Sawhill.

chapters.title: Catching the 2 A.M. Train to Durant
chapters.summary: Clark recalls white children throwing spitballs from their buses and his answering with gravel, the two-teacher grammar school that went through eighth grade, and boarding away for high school: a year in his uncle's home at Mount Olive Vocational High, then enrolling at Holmes County Training School in Durant and stepping off the porch every Monday to catch a 2 a.m. train.

chapters.title: Plowing at Seven Years Old
chapters.summary: Clark describes the family's cows, hogs, chickens, turkeys, geese, horses, and mules, starting a full day in the field plowing at seven when he had to reach over his head for the plow handles, a mule that trotted instead of walking, and a plow that struck a stump and knocked him flat, alongside his resolve not to farm for a living.

chapters.title: Confessed Christ at Three and a Half
chapters.summary: Clark says his aunts farmed the 228-acre family place plus 82 acres he later bought back, and that religion was very important: he confessed Christ at three and a half, was baptized on his mother's assurance he knew what he was doing, and attended Pleasant Green, a small wooden church the congregation could not rebuild because it stood on land a white owner had merely let them use.

chapters.title: Just As Much As Those White Boys
chapters.summary: Clark recalls his mother telling him and his brother, as they rode horses to Ebenezer, that they were just as good as the white boys but the law forbade certain things, his father's father, enslaved in Hinds County until age eleven at emancipation, who described slavery and charged the boy that young men like him would have to grow up and make a difference, the care a Black boy took never to infuriate a white person or look at a white lady, and his lifelong certainty that he was equal, if not as smart, as anybody.

chapters.title: Less Than Five Dollars in My Pocket
chapters.summary: Clark explains he first wanted Tougaloo to study pre-law and become a lawyer like Perry W. Howard, but a work-aid student there would take eight years, so he chose Jackson College, where his father had gone, and left home with a dollar and a quarter and a dollar and fifty cents from relatives, riding the Trailways and Lynch Street buses in with under five dollars.

chapters.title: Bump Your Head Against the Wall
chapters.summary: Clark recounts arriving with no money and being told to go home, sitting a day without food, then being sent to President Jacob L. Reddix, who said you can lead a horse to water but cannot make it drink and arranged custodial work at twenty-five cents an hour plus a garbage job in the women's dormitories to cover tuition, while Clark went out for basketball and track.

chapters.title: Pack Your Clothes and Get Away
chapters.summary: Clark tells how he won Jackson State's first full track scholarship, then how the dean of students, pressing a finger near his nostrils, told him they would not let him use the teaching profession to become a lawyer and that if he wanted law he should leave, so he packed to go home, was never called back, and turned toward teaching since that was all an African American was allowed to do.

chapters.title: I Wasn't Going to Stay Five Years
chapters.summary: Clark describes Dean H.T. Sampson's new health and physical education program that required five chosen students to return a fifth summer, his refusal to stay a fifth year when Sampson would not set up a program for one student, his dropping out at the end of the fall quarter to teach at Sullivan where he had finished high school, and his later return to earn the degree.

chapters.title: Fired for Saying It Was Fine
chapters.summary: Clark recalls the watchword segregation now and segregation forever after the 1954 Brown decision and how anyone who endorsed it was released, illustrated by his seven years teaching at Louise, Mississippi, where, after he told Superintendent Brown that white and black children going to school together was fine, the superintendent walked out and word later came that Clark was no longer needed.

chapters.title: Glad It Did Not Happen to Me
chapters.summary: Clark places the 1955 murder of Emmett Till at the time of a Southwestern Athletic Conference fall workshop, says he was not surprised given what happened to people around Louise, and recounts three men, a quiet parent, a teacher whose wife also taught, and a non-pastor preacher, urging him to stay even after the superintendent did not want him, advice he felt God led him to take.

chapters.title: Shot for Asking People to Register
chapters.topic: The Killing of a Belzoni Man
chapters.summary: Clark tells of teaching at the Humphreys County Training School in the Delta around 1956 and 1957 and a barbershop in Belzoni where a man took the chair after him, then was shot and killed about two hours later for asking people to register and vote, with money said to have been given to send away those who knew something, as the Citizens' Council formed against the Brown decision.

chapters.title: The Black Rabbit and the White Rabbit
chapters.topic: The Sovereignty Commission and a Father Fired
chapters.summary: Clark describes the Citizens' Council and the Mississippi State Sovereignty Commission keeping files and circulating teachers' names to superintendents, a report claiming Humphreys County had gotten rid of all the troublemakers, his own father fired for teaching voter-registration classes and then unable to find work in Mississippi, and a principal at Louise who tried to make Clark pull a library book showing a black rabbit and a white rabbit.

chapters.title: To See If I Measured Up
chapters.topic: Michigan State and the End of Fear
chapters.summary: Clark explains that, taught all his life that ability is not given by race, he applied to Michigan State, Notre Dame, and the University of California to test whether he was equal, choosing Michigan State because California was too far and Notre Dame taught too much religion, since Mississippi offered no place for a Black person to earn a master's, and tells how, the only African American in a class of thirty-five, he answered a question two white classmates had gotten wrong, the professor's confirmation curing him of fear so that being one of twenty-one quiet members never daunted him.

chapters.title: Sending Up the Mileston Fourteen
chapters.summary: Clark recounts coaching at Lexington from 1961 to 1966 as the movement got underway, remembering being sent uptown the day the Mileston group of fourteen tried to register, his father among the last Black names struck from the books, and how, suspected by some Blacks of spying for white people, he entered the movement through the white couple Henry and Sue Lorenzi, who came to Holmes County in the fall of 1964.

chapters.title: Don't Bring No Crowd to the Board
chapters.topic: Freedom Summer and an Adult School Denied
chapters.summary: Clark remembers students joining the movement, one football player punched in the side, the 1964 Freedom Summer that brought about thirty Northern volunteers to Holmes County, and his push for a state-authorized adult education program: told by his superintendent not to bring a crowd, he brought teachers Laplace Polk and Curtis Lodge and warned the board he would run for superintendent, only to have his contract dropped and two legislators pass a local-and-private act, since ruled unconstitutional, letting a county where Highway 12 and Highway 17 intersect appoint its superintendent, which steered him toward a race for the legislature.

chapters.title: You Don't Know How Cruel It Was
chapters.summary: Clark describes Holmes County schools being forced to desegregate in 1965 with only Black students moving into white schools and paying a heavy price, a young woman who later told him at Saints that she would never be the same, and a Durant girl in a rural area whom a superintendent put out of school until Clark and others gave him enough trouble that he did not do it again.

chapters.title: Three Hundred Adults, Six Days a Week
chapters.summary: Clark recounts that after being fired he went to Dr. Arenia C. Mallory at Saints Junior College in Lexington, where she ran 300 adults six days a week, made him coordinator and then director within three months with classes in Pickens, West, and Tchula, and how those adult schools later taught J.P. Love to read and write, undercutting Love's claim that Clark had forged signatures.

chapters.title: The First Integrated Meeting in the County
chapters.summary: Clark describes serving briefly in 1966 on the Lyndon Johnson Great Society community action board, the county's first integrated meeting at the courthouse with Blacks and whites around the table joining Central Mississippi Inc., and the hundred gallons of red Kool-Aid he made because Black folk had never before been able to drink red Kool-Aid there.

chapters.title: Head Start Ought to Be Pre-K
chapters.topic: The Cause and the CAP Program
chapters.summary: Clark, unhappy with the board's direction, insists the cause is more important than any individual and drafts a letter from the Freedom Democratic Party of Holmes County telling elected officials to do their job or resign, then traces the CAP program from a social service into a jobs program and his work with Rust College on night school for Head Start teachers, arguing Head Start should be a pre-K program so children do not start school behind.

chapters.title: Let Them Know I Am One of You
chapters.summary: Clark explains he volunteered to run after the superintendent office was made appointive, and credits his win to presenting himself as one of the people rather than a big shot, to the strong Holmes County civil rights movement, and above all to his contact as director with the 300-plus adults at the migrant-farmer school, whom he let in late after a long day and who helped put him over.

chapters.title: When Durant Came In
chapters.topic: An Independent Wins a Contested Seat
chapters.summary: Clark recounts running as an independent in 1967 because Black candidates could not be Democrats and reasoning the people might not return a second time, facing J.P. Love after Love beat Wilbur Hooks in a redistricted single seat, the contest that ran all the way with Marian Wright as his lead lawyer, the unofficial watchers of God he posted at every voting place so Black voters would not be intimidated, and the count when he trailed until the Durant votes, organized by an ally he calls Jumbo Brouche, put him over.

chapters.title: One Black Among a Hundred Twenty-One
chapters.summary: Clark recalls Marian Wright crossing the Capitol's south side to tell him the House had decided to seat him, says the experience of being one Black member among 121 white ones did not intimidate him because he knew education better than anyone there, and explains the seating rules under which his delegation's senior member, then the speaker, chose where he would sit, leaving him eight years with an empty seat beside him.

chapters.title: You're Doing What They Want You to Do
chapters.summary: Clark describes being denied the floor as the speaker recognized others to cut him out, walking out one night with his desk cleared until Bill Minor said he was doing exactly what they wanted, at which Clark dropped his hand and returned to a hooraying chamber that fell silent, and how the preparation habits he built at Michigan State let him master coming legislation by burning the midnight hour.

chapters.title: Point to My Nostrils to Vote No
chapters.topic: Earning a Vote After Eight Years
chapters.summary: Clark recounts that after about eight years a group of Black legislators was elected from Jackson and the older white gentlemen who had opposed seating him began looking to him at vote time, so he signaled how to vote with a finger up for aye or a point to his nostrils for no, never directing them wrong even when fellow Black folk called him an Uncle Tom, and a white seatmate who feared Clark was trying to get him defeated.

chapters.title: A White Man on His Knee
chapters.summary: Clark tells how, tired of following so-called liberal friends who got good appointments while he got nothing, he signed the petition that gave C.B. Buddie Newman the majority he needed for Speaker Pro Tem when Speaker John R. Junkin fell sick, and how Newman dropped to a knee and kissed the pen, marveling that a white man knelt, to which Clark answered he would do much more for Black folk in the future.

chapters.title: Potential Negro Chairman Gives Black Power Speech
chapters.topic: A Keynote and a Vice Chairmanship
chapters.summary: Clark recounts Newman making him vice chairman of education after Junkin died, his NAACP convention keynote in Vicksburg, in Newman's district, telling Black folk to get up and do for themselves, the bold headline calling it a Black Power speech, and Newman summoning him to show three boxes of papers yet promising to approach him when an education chairmanship came vacant, which changed Clark's attitude toward him.

chapters.title: Get My Bills Out First
chapters.topic: Building the Reform Bill With Governor Winter
chapters.summary: Clark, now chairman, describes a white Lexington lawyer tipping him to what was coming on the floor, his long push for progressive education dating to a bill killed the day he went to Dr. King's funeral in Atlanta, and how after William Winter became governor they assembled the education reform bill, which Clark got out of committee in the first week even as the Rules Committee repeatedly kicked it back to the heel of the calendar.

chapters.title: There Ain't Supposed to Be No Boys
chapters.topic: Forcing the Bill to the Floor
chapters.summary: Clark recounts telling the speaker he would call the bill to the top of the calendar, the speaker warning some of these boys could not stand the heat and refusing to recognize him, his answer that any boys should go home, his vice chairman vowing to make the same motion, the House erupting until it had to adjourn, and the anonymous letters threatening colleagues' wives and families if they voted for the bill.

chapters.title: Take It to the People
chapters.summary: Clark describes telling Governor Winter he could not keep putting his members through the bloodbath without support and urging him to take it to old-line organizations like the Mississippi Southern Baptists and the Farm Bureau, the governor's Greenville meeting drawing crowds Clark could not get within three blocks of, and Winter summoning six legislators to the mansion to announce he would set a date for a press conference on a special session.

chapters.title: I Put Myself Into a Jail
chapters.summary: Clark tells the speaker and lieutenant governor that if not removed he will get a bill out and passed once Winter calls the session, then chairs the conference committee where Ellis Bodron's blind son maneuvers him, starting with Clark, into conceding a half-percent tax after he vowed to accept none, leaving the bill tied up three weeks as opponents, including a Grenada member the speaker added, tried to kill it.

chapters.title: Have the Senator Make My Speech
chapters.topic: Saving the Bill and What It Did
chapters.summary: Clark recounts Newman warning that long meetings tend to kill legislation, his stratagem of quietly arming a senator to argue his no-tax position back to him because he believed the money already existed and taxes were a way to kill the bill, its passage as the Education Reform Act restoring compulsory school attendance repealed since around the Brown decision and adding early childhood kindergarten, and an earlier committee tactic of hiding members in the cloakroom, closet, and bathroom to break opponents' walkout and force the bill to the top of the calendar.

chapters.title: Fight for the Farmers
chapters.topic: The First Run Against Webb Franklin
chapters.summary: Clark explains he ran for Congress to fight for farmers without the mentality that nothing helpful could come to the Delta, notes the long power of Delta planters and his friendships with figures like John Stennis and James Eastland in a time when important Democrats backed him, then describes his well-funded opponent Webb Franklin, the low turnout in Holmes County because he would not embrace the indicted Tchula mayor Eddie Carthan and instead let the courts operate, and Franklin's slogan he's one of us, which Clark calls racism played to white people.

chapters.title: I'm Running Anyway
chapters.summary: Clark recounts declining to run for Speaker Pro Tem the first time because some African Americans would not back him and he did not want grandchildren asked why they refused a Black man, then running the second time and, with 17 of 35 African American votes, winning 87 percent of the whole Mississippi House, members who saw he was Black but voted for him as a person, and his refusal to spend money to spin a budget.

chapters.title: If the Delta Farmers Had Supported Me
chapters.topic: The Second Congressional Race and Redistricting
chapters.summary: Clark says his second run against Franklin was much the same though better organized, that he would have won, especially the second time, had Delta farmers backed him, that he was identified with the civil rights movement while the Black candidate who eventually won was not, and that the district was gerrymandered against Black voters until later made overwhelmingly Black, which made it harder for Democrats to win another seat.

chapters.title: My Education Was Important
chapters.topic: Family and the Boys He Raised
chapters.summary: Clark, elected Speaker Pro Tem in 1992 among a growing number of Black representatives, turns to family: married in 1971 around age 38, he helped raise and educate his sister's children, including a nephew who became a young cardiologist in California after the University of Michigan, and taught his own two sons, both now lawyers, that they were somebody and that education mattered.

chapters.title: Government by the Party, Not for the People
chapters.summary: Asked about problems and changes, Clark names political parties as Mississippi's greatest hindrance, governing by party rather than for the people as in Washington, calls the freedom to go, eat, and stay anywhere the greatest change since the civil rights movement, and warns that without action history may record that for lack of leadership the movement's good works died, work he says he is still doing.

loc_video.caption: Robert G. Clark, Jr., oral history interview conducted by John Dittmer in Pickens, Mississippi, 2013 March 13
